title: What is on the horizon for the GB energy sector in February 2026? - Dentons
description:
Ten years ago, the GB electricity sector had just learnt to love the Coalition Government's Electricity Market Reform programme, in particular Contracts for Difference (CfDs) as a revenue support mechanism for renewables and the Capacity Market to bolster the economics of flexible plant.
For the first time, there will be quite a detailed blueprint for how much of the various types of generation, storage and network infrastructure is needed, where and when, to operate an efficient and economical electricity sector.
GBE and NWF deal announcements are a reminder of how the government looks to energy projects as a source of both emissions reductions and growth in the form of "good quality jobs".
The recent Advanced Nuclear Framework is a firm statement of intent to use state financial support and regulatory reform to achieve a GB nuclear renaissance.
Regulation and finance are the life blood of energy sector projects.
text:
Ten years ago, the GB electricity sector had just learnt to love the Coalition Government's Electricity Market Reform programme, in particular Contracts for Difference (CfDs) as a revenue support mechanism for renewables and the Capacity Market to bolster the economics of flexible plant. At the same time, cost pressures were accelerating the closure of legacy renewables support schemes and a new era of more muscular, directive regulation for the UK upstream oil and gas sector beckoned with reforms aiming to maximise the economic recovery of petroleum from the UK Continental Shelf.

A year ago, all eyes were on the new government's Clean Power Action Plan 2030 and how its ambitions for a decarbonised grid would be affected by whether it chose to reorganise GB electricity wholesale markets on a zonal basis under the Review of Electricity Market Arrangements (REMA).

In the last 10 years, and the last 12 months, much has changed, and much has stayed the same.

Ministers decided not to pursue zonal wholesale pricing. In its place, we have "Reformed National Policy" (RNP) which is built around the Strategic Spatial Energy Plan (SSEP), the first triennial iteration of which NESO is expected to finalise in 2027. For the first time, there will be quite a detailed blueprint for how much of the various types of generation, storage and network infrastructure is needed, where and when, to operate an efficient and economical electricity sector. The question is how to shape the existing and future pipelines of projects to realise the vision set out in the SSEP.

The answer, to be explored in the forthcoming RNP delivery plan, is by using a range of regulatory "levers" to influence developers' investment decisions: planning reform, seabed leasing, network development, CfDs and other support regimes, grid connection management, and network charging. Consultations on some of these topics, or on the SSEP, can seem a bit arid, but anybody seeking to commission projects from 2030 on – or trying to hedge their portfolios against specific potential regulatory outcomes in the nearer term – should keep a close eye on them over the next year.

Two examples of such levers in the revenue support category are CfDs and the cap and floor scheme for long duration (eight hours or more discharging at rated capacity) energy storage (LDES) projects.

The CfD allocation process and contractual framework have evolved since 2014 into much more than just an auction process designed to keep subsidy costs down, backed by a lengthy payments contract. Under REMA, the government decided not to reform CfDs fundamentally, but has opted to continue making significant incremental changes. After the success of AR7, new features being considered for AR8 include further developments to the clean investment bonus scheme, which may become more explicit about workforce outcomes (net zero jobs should be good jobs) and applies to onshore as well as offshore wind for the first time, and the introduction of hybrid metering. This is a technical solution that could significantly enhance CfDs' flexibility of operation, potentially enabling capacity within a single Balancing Mechanism Unit to combine operation on a merchant basis and under a CfD.

The LDES cap and floor regime, by contrast, is still working towards the conclusion of its first allocation round, seeking to provide a secure basis for the financing of large and capital-intensive infrastructure whose services will certainly be needed in a renewables-dominated grid, but whose revenues could be quite unpredictable, meaning that the combination of a regulated revenue floor, calibrated by Ofgem through project-specific assessment and value-for-money considerations, with a corresponding ceiling in the form of the cap, is attractive. The framework under which Ofgem is assessing projects is flexible and it will be very interesting to see how much weight is given to qualitative factors alongside those based purely on price.

Like LDES, the strategic location of major sources of power demand, such as data centres, can also play a key part in improving the efficiency of grid operation and the viability of individual generation projects. However, data centres are also central to the AI side of the growth agenda. Over the last year, a series of steps have been taken to recognise these things in planning policy documents, such as the National Planning Policy Framework, and to give them the opportunity to seek planning consent directly from central government as "nationally significant infrastructure projects". However, at the same time, developer enthusiasm has produced a significant "data centre bubble" in the reformed grid connections queue. How will Ofgem and NESO develop the tools they have created to manage generation connections to ensure that the "right" projects are prioritised for connection?

Over the last year, the current government's plans for using state-backed commercial finance in and around the energy sector has come more clearly into focus, with primary legislation establishing Great British Energy (GBE), and both GBE and the National Wealth Fund (NWF) now having published strategic plans that make it very clear what kinds of project they are interested in. The past 12 months have seen examples across the sector of these organisations deploying debt or equity finance to accelerate strategic projects and get them better terms from other lenders/investors: from new nuclear to onshore and offshore wind, and EV charging to major subsea transmission upgrades.

GBE and NWF deal announcements are a reminder of how the government looks to energy projects as a source of both emissions reductions and growth in the form of "good quality jobs". Nowhere is that more so than in the case of nuclear, which crucially also provides clean power that is "firm". Nuclear is being strongly supported in all its forms: large conventional projects (Sizewell C achieved FID and financial close in 2025, significant life-extension of Sizewell B is proposed), all forms of modular nuclear technology (now collectively referred to as "advanced nuclear") and even steps towards a prototype fusion plant by 2040. The recent Advanced Nuclear Framework is a firm statement of intent to use state financial support and regulatory reform to achieve a GB nuclear renaissance.

As ever, security of supply and affordability are never far from policymakers' minds. DESNZ has some quite striking proposals for Capacity Market reforms that could come into force in the next T-4 auction, including access to prices above the usual £70/kW/year threshold for some bidders. More radically still, DESNZ's recent consultation on security of gas supply appears to contemplate some potentially significant state interventions in storage or other infrastructure provision, or market activity, to ensure that the country's energy system is not unduly exposed to future availability or price shocks.

Whereas 10 years ago, official talk was of revitalising the North Sea upstream sector, current policy is dominated by the decision that there should be "no new licences", but it seems likely that the details of that policy and the expected exceptions to it will be keenly debated when the anticipated Energy Independence Bill reaches Parliament. However, newer kinds of North Sea project, such as international collaborations on offshore wind and interconnection, as envisaged by the recent Hamburg Declaration, may benefit from the proposed return of the UK to the EU single electricity market. Negotiations on this, and the linking of the UK ETS to the EU ETS, are ongoing and could bring significant benefits to those trading electricity, carbon or physical CO2 across borders.

Perhaps less spectacularly, but no less crucially for the achievement of energy policy goals, the steady work of maintaining and developing a range of regulatory structures goes on in a more or less business-as-usual way. Under the Energy Act 2023, reforms to the governance of the industry codes that sit at the heart of the GB energy industry are moving forward to enable them to be more strongly directed as instruments of reform, without losing the benefits of close industry involvement in their workings. Similarly, the long-discussed possibility of introducing full competition into the provision of strategic transmission projects onshore (known as CATOs) is now almost a reality.

Finally a series of consultations has been issued with a view to establishing new or tweaking existing regulatory frameworks to facilitate the development of low carbon hydrogen and CCUS projects in GB. Topics covered include access to infrastructure, use of the RAB model, hydrogen to power, blending and more. And, as if all that was not enough, we are seeing both the impact of the first wave of obligations on aviation fuel suppliers to purchase sustainable aviation fuel (SAF, at this stage all bio-based) and the development of new revenue support models as the UK and EU seek to close the bankability gap to allow both current and future technologies of SAF production (including hydrogen-based "power to liquids" fuels) to scale up.

Regulation and finance are the life blood of energy sector projects. We are advising government, regulators, system operators, developers or finance providers on all the above areas. Please get in touch if you would like to discuss any of them with our team, who live and breathe energy regulation.

title: Sustainable Aviation Fuel Is Taking Flight, But Significant Headwinds Remain, According to ...
description:
The report reveals that SAF buyers share clear, common characteristics: SAF purchasing is on the rise.
Twenty percent of travel programs are purchasing SAF certificates to abate business travel emissions in 2025, a 30% increase from the previous year.
About the GBTA Foundation The GBTA Foundation is the non-profit, purpose-driven arm of the Global Business Travel Association.
Our mission is to transform business travel into a powerful force for a sustainable, inclusive, and equitable future for People and the Planet.
We achieve this through the world’s largest network of business travel professionals driving collective action through advocacy, standard-setting, talent development, and capacity-building.
text:
New insights show which corporate sectors are leading early SAF uptake as well as the enablers and challenges driving SAF adoption as a significant lever to reduce business travel emissions

Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) purchasing for business travel is on a fast upward trajectory, according to new research released today by the GBTA Foundation’s Acceleration Challenge. Global travel programs with Net Zero commitments are leading the way in SAF adoption, enabled by strong emissions management, internal carbon pricing and flexible purchasing options. The GBTA research also highlights the biggest barriers slowing broader uptake, including uncertainty around accounting standards, cost sensitivity and limited internal readiness.

Offering the most comprehensive insights to date of corporate behavior around SAF, the GBTA report titled “Corporate Behavior on Sustainable Aviation Fuel Purchases,” draws on data from 58 SAF‑purchasing organizations who participated in the GBTA Sustainability Acceleration Challenge. As the global business travel sector works to decarbonize, SAF remains the most viable near‑term lever for reducing aviation emissions.

Unveiling the report at the Sustainable Aviation Futures MENA Congress in Dubai, GBTA Senior Vice President, Advocacy and Sustainability, Delphine Millot, who also oversees the GBTA Foundation, said, “With clearer guidance, lower barriers to entry, and stronger education, many more companies around the world could begin the journey towards incorporating SAF into their decarbonization strategies.”

Who Is Purchasing SAF Today?

The report reveals that SAF buyers share clear, common characteristics:

SAF purchasing is on the rise. Twenty percent of travel programs are purchasing SAF certificates to abate business travel emissions in 2025, a 30% increase from the previous year. There is also an “intent ready” segment of buyers (11%) who plan to purchase within a year.

Companies with a high reliance on travel and strong Net Zero commitments are leading adoption. Companies in technology, transportation and travel services, and pharmaceutical and life sciences represent the bulk of current buyers, as industries with high reliance on air travel and heightened expectations for climate leadership.

Large, global travel programs dominate. Organizations with more than 10,000 employees or over $50 million in annual travel spend account for the majority of today’s SAF purchases, reflecting their greater internal sustainability infrastructure, governance and resources. A significant share of SAF purchasers operate global travel programs, benefiting from centralized policies, harmonized reporting, and multi‑market oversight that make SAF procurement more feasible.

As the report notes: “SAF adoption is not random. It aligns strongly with company size, emissions visibility, and the maturity of sustainability programs.”

What Enables SAF Adoption?

The research identifies three primary enablers that consistently distinguish SAF buyers from non‑buyers:

Strong emissions tracking, reporting and target setting. Companies that purchase SAF overwhelmingly track and report Scope 3.6 business‑travel emissions—and many have specific internal reduction targets. These practices help quantify impact, strengthen the business case, and create accountability that drives action.

Internal carbon pricing as a practical finance tool. Twenty percent of SAF buyers use internal carbon pricing, with an average internal fee of $95 per ton of CO₂e. This mechanism translates emissions into financial terms, generates funds to support procurement, and signals organizational commitment.

Flexible, accessible purchasing channels. More than half of buyers purchase SAF through airline direct programs, which reduce friction by streamlining contracting and providing reliable emissions data. Others diversify through fuel producers, retailers, buyer alliances, or travel management companies (TMCs)—proof that multiple on‑ramps exist for companies beginning their SAF journey.

What’s Holding Companies Back?

While SAF interest is rising, adoption is still limited. The report highlights key barriers:

Cost sensitivity and low willingness to pay. Most companies—SAF buyers and non‑buyers alike—are unwilling to pay more for sustainable travel options although a 5%-10% airfare increase can materially support SAF production.

Uncertainty around standards and future counting rules. Nearly half of companies remain unsure whether SAF certificates will count toward emissions‑reduction targets. Recent draft guidance from climate‑standard setters may help, but previous ambiguity has slowed long‑term planning.

Limited internal readiness among smaller programs. Companies without established emissions tracking, centralized travel governance, or sustainability budgets often lack the foundational tools needed to initiate SAF procurement.

A Pivotal Moment for SAF Market Acceleration

In 2025, the GBTA Foundation launched SAF Corporate Connect. This initiative is intended to provide a forum for the business travel community to share knowledge, experience and insights around SAF.

“While our research shows that the proportion of companies currently purchasing or intending to purchase SAF is increasing, it will only be through sustained corporate demand that SAF finally begins to take off as a viable solution, moving from the realm of ambition to that of reality,” emphasized Millot.

Read the full GBTA Foundation SAF Insights report here.

Find out more about how your company can participate in the next GBTA Sustainability Acceleration Challenge.

About the GBTA Foundation

The GBTA Foundation is the non-profit, purpose-driven arm of the Global Business Travel Association. The GBTA Foundation is a leading force that empowers a resilient future for individuals and teams who connect the world through business travel. Our mission is to transform business travel into a powerful force for a sustainable, inclusive, and equitable future for People and the Planet. We achieve this through the world’s largest network of business travel professionals driving collective action through advocacy, standard-setting, talent development, and capacity-building. GBTAFoundation.org

h a warrant exercisable at $0.15 for four years. If the acquisition closes in March 2026, CDL’s executive chair Kaush Rakhit will join Cielo’s board, and CDL principals will be under a two-year standstill on selling and certain takeover moves.

Why should I care?

For markets: Dilution is the price of staying in the game.

This is a familiar micro-cap energy financing combo – stock to buy expertise, then more equity-linked paper to fund the next milestones. The key question is whether the acquired tools actually shorten feasibility work and de-risk Project Nexus enough to unlock larger partners, grants, or debt later. The two-year standstill may reduce near-term selling pressure, which can matter a lot in thinly traded names.

The bigger picture: Clean fuels need proof before they get big money.

Sustainable aviation fuel often hits a “prove it” gap: banks and strategic buyers want solid feedstock analysis, techno-economic modeling, and permitting progress before committing capital. Cielo says the raise will fund early engineering, regulatory and incentive applications, and working capital in Prince George – a typical pre-construction checklist. Bringing the seller onto the board can help execution and accountability, but it also puts timelines and governance under a brighter spotlight.

title: DLR at ECATS 2026: Research for climate-compatible aviation
description:
The three-day event organised by the ECATS International Association brought together research and practice to reduce the climate impact of aviation.
Global emissions assessments A key focus was the presentation of DLR modelling environments that enable the assessment of aviation climate mitigation measures.
With the Digital Hangar, the DLR Institute of Systems Architectures in Aeronautics demonstrates what future aircraft might look like.
The research emphasises that appropriate incentives for airports and airlines, as well as suitable infrastructure, are necessary for an efficient reduction of aviation’s climate impact.3 Simple estimation of the climate impact of individual flights With FlightClim 2.0, the Institute presented a Python tool developed with the DLR Institute of Atmospheric Physics that simplifies the calculation of the climate impact of individual flights.
2 Niklaß, M., Dahlmann, K., Grunau, R., Lührs, B., Völk, S., Grewe, V. “Aviation Mitigation Pathways: Temperature Responses from Emissions Scenario Modeling”, 5th ECATS Conference 2026, Brussels, Belgium.
text:
February 11, 2026

ECATS 2026 – New models and methods for assessing and mitigating the climate impact of aviation

Efficient emissions modelling

Targeted use of sustainable aviation fuels at airports

Simplified estimation of the climate effects of individual flights

Operational procedures such as wake energy retrieval

The Institute of Air Transport of the German Aerospace Center (DLR) was represented with numerous presentations and posters at the 5th ECATS Conference in Brussels. The three-day event organised by the ECATS International Association brought together research and practice to reduce the climate impact of aviation. In addition to presenting new scientific findings, discussions focused on practical solutions for airports, airlines and technology developers. The tools and analyses presented by the Institute provide a transparent basis for systematically assessing operational measures and technologies and for making aviation more sustainable in the long term.

Global emissions assessments

A key focus was the presentation of DLR modelling environments that enable the assessment of aviation climate mitigation measures. This requires a system-wide approach that models how the choice of technologies, fuels and operational concepts affects aviation emissions and what consequences this has for global temperature development. Global emissions and climate models are particularly computationally intensive. Fast surrogate models are therefore indispensable for evaluating and comparing different mitigation pathways. The approach presented uses the DLR models GRIDcast, OpenAirClim and the Digital Hangar.

GRIDcast is an efficient 3D emissions calculator developed by the DLR Institute of Air Transport that estimates the spatial and temporal evolution of global aviation emissions for future market and technology scenarios. GRIDcast is based on high-resolution, pre-calculated emissions inventories and simulates technology and demand developments by region, market and technology. This enables researchers to generate realistic scenarios up to 2050 in a short time without having to perform complex simulations for each individual flight route. GRIDcast is therefore a scalable and flexible response model that can estimate the temporal development of aviation emissions resulting from future decisions on technologies, fuels and operational concepts.1

Complementing this, the DLR Institute of Atmospheric Physics provides OpenAirClim, an open climate response model that calculates the impacts of aviation emissions from radiative forcing through to expected temperature changes. Such models are particularly important for quickly assessing the climate impact of non-CO2 effects, as these depend strongly on the location and timing of emissions and can only be reliably represented through non-linear relationships.

With the Digital Hangar, the DLR Institute of Systems Architectures in Aeronautics demonstrates what future aircraft might look like. The open aircraft database provides detailed descriptions of performance, systems and geometry data for potential configurations. In addition to evolutionary developments of existing designs, it also includes innovative concept aircraft with hydrogen or hybrid-electric propulsion.

Using the combined capabilities of GRIDcast, OpenAirClim and the Digital Hangar enables the temporal assessment of climate mitigation measures – from individual actions to complete mitigation pathways that take into account various operational, technological and regulatory developments.2

Targeted use of sustainable fuels

Another contribution focused on Sustainable Aviation Fuels (SAF). Analyses using Copenhagen Airport as a case study within the ALIGHT project show that targeted distribution of SAF to flights with particularly high climate impact can significantly reduce CO2-equivalent emissions while also being economically advantageous. The research emphasises that appropriate incentives for airports and airlines, as well as suitable infrastructure, are necessary for an efficient reduction of aviation’s climate impact.3

Simple estimation of the climate impact of individual flights

With FlightClim 2.0, the Institute presented a Python tool developed with the DLR Institute of Atmospheric Physics that simplifies the calculation of the climate impact of individual flights. It uses inputs such as departure and destination airports and aircraft weight to estimate both the climate impact of CO2 emissions and non-CO2 effects per flight and per passenger. The current version is based on a global dataset of actual flight trajectories from 2019, emissions inventories from the DLR project ELK, and derived climate impact estimates. It can be used in research, for monitoring climate footprints, and in travel planning to support more sustainable decisions.4

Reducing climate impact through innovative operational concepts

Another topic was the Wake Energy Retrieval (WER) procedure, also known as formation flight. In this concept, a following aircraft deliberately uses the upwash generated by the wake vortices of a leading aircraft to reduce its own fuel consumption. The study presented within the SESAR 3 JU project GEESE investigates possible WER operations involving two or three aircraft in European air traffic based on real traffic data. It considers not only the achievable savings in CO2 emissions, but also the effects of WER operations on non-CO2 effects such as nitrogen oxide emissions and contrail formation. Saturation effects lead to increased climate mitigation potential, particularly in formations of three aircraft. Such operational measures could make an important contribution to reducing the climate impact of aviation in the future.5

1 Grunau, R., Lührs, B., Niklaß, M. “GRIDcast: Efficient Modeling of Aviation Emissions for Future Markets and Technology Scenarios”, 5th ECATS Conference 2026, Brussels, Belgium.

2 Niklaß, M., Dahlmann, K., Grunau, R., Lührs, B., Völk, S., Grewe, V. “Aviation Mitigation Pathways: Temperature Responses from Emissions Scenario Modeling”, 5th ECATS Conference 2026, Brussels, Belgium.

3 Müller, L., Kumar, S., Grimme, W., Maertens, S., Eichinger, R., Enderle, B., Weder, C., Dahlmann, K., Grewe, V., “Targeted Use of Sustainable Aviation Fuel at Airports for Climate Mitigation: A Cost-Benefit Perspective”, 5th ECATS Conference 2026, Brussels, Belgium.

text: Sustainable aviation fuels #SAF are currently the only realistically scalable solution for making aviation significantly more climate-friendly over the next 20 to 30 years – without completely new aircraft technology. Extremely exciting conference with very interesting #companies, #entrepreneurs, #investors and #policy_makers. Thanks to Sustainable Aviation Futures Fraunhofer UMSICHT is able to push the realization with a multitude of technological options: •. #CO2 to #methanol -> Carbon2Chem (see below) •. #Methanol to #fuel •. #Low_temperature #CO2_electrolysis •. #System_evaluation and #eco_system_building Several key issues need to be resolved in order to establish SAF (Sustainable Aviation Fuel) on a broad scale. The challenges are technical, economic, and political: 1. High production costs 💰 • SAF is currently 2–5 times more expensive than fossil kerosene. • Reasons: small production volumes, expensive raw materials, complex processes. • Solution: scaling, technological innovation, government subsidies. 2. Limited production capacities 🏭 • SAF currently covers only a very small proportion of global kerosene demand. • There is a lack of large refineries and long-term investment security. • Expansion requires billions in investment. 3. Raw material availability 🌱 SAF can be produced from various sources: • Used cooking oils & fats (HEFA process) • Biomass • Agricultural residues • Synthetically from CO₂ + green hydrogen (power-to-liquid) Problem: • Waste oils are in limited supply • Biomass competes with food production • E-fuels require a lot of renewable electricity 4. Green hydrogen & electricity demand ⚡ For synthetic SAF (E-SAF): • Very high demand for renewable energy • Green hydrogen is still expensive and scarce •. Infrastructure for electrolysis must be massively expanded 5. Infrastructure & logistics ✈️ • Adaptation of supply chains • Certification & blending rules • Refueling and storage infrastructure at airports 6. Political framework conditions 📜 • Clear quotas (e.g., EU ReFuelEU Aviation) • CO₂ pricing •. Subsidy programs • Long-term investment security Without political incentives, companies often do not invest on a large scale 7. Proof of sustainability 🌍 • Ensuring that SAF actually saves CO₂ • Avoiding “greenwashing” • Life cycle analysis (from raw material to combustion) In short: SAF is technically feasible, but still too expensive, too scarce, and too energy-intensive to completely replace fossil kerosene. The key lies in: • #Scaling • #Affordable_green_electricity • #Political_incentives • #Technological_progress Fraunhofer UMSICHT and Fraunhofer-Gesellschaft will #combine_forces to provide #technological_support and #technolgies for the #transformation.

title: Why The Airbus A321XLR Is Such A Breakthrough For Aviation - Simple Flying
description:
The Airbus A321XLR is considered a game-changer in modern aviation because it represents a meaningful step forward in what modern aircraft are designed to do, without radically changing the way airlines operate.
Join us as we explore what makes this aircraft so favorable to airlines, shaping the next phase of commercial aviation.
The A321XLR range allows nonstop flights lasting 10-11 hours, enabling direct services between continents using a narrowbody platform.
With quieter cabins, improved lighting, and modern seating options, many travelers find the onboard experience more than acceptable for long flights.
By pairing long-range capability with strong efficiency and lower emissions, the A321XLR allows airlines to grow their networks more responsibly.
text:
The Airbus A321XLR is considered a game-changer in modern aviation because it represents a meaningful step forward in what modern aircraft are designed to do, without radically changing the way airlines operate. It builds on an already proven platform while extending its capabilities in a way that expands airline options. Rather than introducing an entirely new category of aircraft, the A321XLR refines an existing one, showing how incremental design changes can produce significant operational benefits.

Its importance lies in how it quietly broadens route possibilities and improves efficiency for airlines, without demanding major shifts in infrastructure or strategy. The aircraft enables carriers to reassess certain routes and fleet decisions with greater flexibility, while maintaining the economics and operational familiarity of a narrowbody aircraft. Join us as we explore what makes this aircraft so favorable to airlines, shaping the next phase of commercial aviation.

Unmatched Long-Range Capability In A Single-Aisle Aircraft

The Airbus A321XLR redefines what a single-aisle aircraft can do by stretching its range to approximately 4,700 nautical miles (8,700 km), a figure that was once firmly in widebody territory, except for maybe the longest range 757–200, which was modified with winglets to achieve a higher range of up to 4,100 nm (7,600 km). The A321XLR range allows nonstop flights lasting 10-11 hours, enabling direct services between continents using a narrowbody platform. Routes that previously required a connection or a larger aircraft can now be flown economically with fewer seats and lower risk.

This performance is enabled by structural and system changes rather than simple add-on fuel tanks. The aircraft features a permanently integrated rear center fuel tank, an increased maximum takeoff weight of roughly 223,000 lbs (101,000 kg), and aerodynamic refinements that preserve efficiency at long distances. These upgrades allow the aircraft to carry enough fuel for extended missions without sacrificing payload flexibility.

As a result, airlines gain the ability to connect distant city pairs that were never viable before. Instead of concentrating on long-haul travel through major hubs, carriers can extrapolate the surge in popularity and demand in operating nonstop flights between smaller or secondary cities, fundamentally expanding global connectivity while keeping aircraft size aligned with demand.

A Significant Change In Airline Economics

One of the A321XLR’s most disruptive qualities is its cost structure. Compared with older mid-size widebody aircraft, it delivers around 30% lower fuel burn per seat, significantly reducing operating costs on long sectors. This matters in an industry where fuel often represents 20–30% of total airline expenses, especially on long-haul routes.

The lower trip cost means airlines no longer need to fill 250-300 seats to make a long-distance route viable. Instead, they can profitably operate flights with closer to 180-220 seats, significantly reducing the break-even load factor. As a result, routes that would previously have been too risky become financially sustainable, even when passenger demand is modest, highly seasonal, or still developing over time.

By better matching aircraft size to actual passenger demand, the A321XLR allows airlines to reduce financial risk while still improving profitability. Instead of committing to large jets that are difficult to fill consistently, airlines can operate long-range routes with an aircraft that carries fewer seats but costs less to fly. This makes it easier to launch new routes, adjust capacity as demand changes, and avoid the losses that come from flying oversized planes with too many empty seats.

A Catalyst For New Route Strategies

The A321XLR is reshaping airline network planning by making long-haul point-to-point flying more practical than ever before. Instead of routing passengers through large hubs, airlines can now link smaller cities directly across oceans or continents. This reduces total travel time and increases convenience, both of which are highly valued by passengers.

From a competitive standpoint, this capability enables airlines to enter markets with limited competition and establish nonstop services where rivals cannot justify operating widebody aircraft. These routes may not support daily widebody flights, but they can thrive with smaller aircraft flying fewer frequencies at lower cost.

Over time, this model leads to more diversified global networks. Rather than concentrating growth in already congested hub airports, airlines can distribute capacity across a broader range of cities and secondary markets. This approach improves network resilience by reducing exposure to disruptions at any single hub, while also lowering dependency on a small number of major airports and creating more balanced, flexible route structures.

Long-Haul Comfort In A Narrowbody Cabin

While narrowbody aircraft have traditionally been used for short flights, the A321XLR is designed to keep passengers comfortable on journeys that can last nearly 10-11 hours. Airlines such as Aer Lingus and Iberia plan to use the aircraft on transatlantic routes, relying on its newer cabin design with improved lighting, quieter interiors, and much larger overhead bins to make long flights more comfortable, even with a single aisle.

The A321XLR is also capable of featuring lie-flat business class seats, which are essential for airlines targeting corporate and high-yield travelers. Carriers like American Airlines and Icelandair have highlighted the importance of pairing these premium seats with modern inflight entertainment and fast onboard connectivity. Together, these features allow airlines to deliver a true long-haul experience on routes that do not justify a widebody, while still meeting the expectations of business travelers and premium leisure passengers.

Passenger acceptance, and in some cases, clear preference, is critical to the A321XLR’s success, and early feedback suggests that its comfort improvements significantly narrow the experience gap between single-aisle and twin-aisle aircraft. With quieter cabins, improved lighting, and modern seating options, many travelers find the onboard experience more than acceptable for long flights. For a growing number of passengers, a nonstop journey on a smaller aircraft is actually preferable to a longer trip involving connections, layovers, and terminal changes, even if it means flying on a narrowbody.

Filling A Critical Gap In The Aircraft Market

For years, airlines have searched for a replacement aircraft that could sit between large narrowbodies and smaller widebodies, capable of flying long distances without carrying more seats than the market can support. The A321XLR fits this role almost perfectly, offering a range of about 4,700 nautical miles (8,700 km) while maintaining single-aisle operating costs. In doing so, it effectively replaces aircraft like the Boeing 757, which once filled this niche but is no longer in production and is becoming increasingly costly for airlines to operate as fleets age.

The lack of a true competitor has amplified the A321XLR’s impact. Boeing’s closest alternative, the Boeing 737 MAX 10, is still awaiting certification and, even once approved, will be limited by a significantly shorter range of roughly 3,300 nautical miles (6,100 km). That range difference means the MAX 10 is best suited for high-density domestic routes or short international flights, while the A321XLR can handle missions such as transatlantic services that push close to 10-11 hours. As a result, the two aircraft do not compete evenly in the long-range segment Airbus now dominates.

Certification delays for the 737 MAX 10 have further strengthened Airbus’s position. Airlines planning long-term fleet replacements cannot afford uncertainty, especially as older jets retire, and many have committed to the A321XLR simply because no comparable Boeing option is available in the near term. With hundreds of orders already placed, the aircraft has become the go-to solution for long, thin routes that once required widebodies. By filling this long-standing gap, the A321XLR has secured a strategic advantage that is likely to influence airline fleet decisions for decades.

Designed For A More Sustainable Future

Environmental performance has become a major factor in how new aircraft are designed, and the A321XLR reflects that shift clearly. Its high fuel efficiency leads directly to lower CO₂ emissions per seat, especially on long, thinner routes where a widebody aircraft would often fly with many empty seats. Using a smaller, more efficient aircraft on these routes helps airlines avoid burning excess fuel simply to move unused capacity, reducing emissions without cutting connectivity.

The A321XLR is also certified to operate with up to 50% Sustainable Aviation Fuel(SAF) today, and it has been designed with the expectation that 100% SAF capability will become standard as fuel supply expands and regulations evolve. This gives airlines flexibility to reduce lifecycle emissions as cleaner fuels become more widely available, without needing to replace aircraft prematurely. For carriers under pressure to meet long-term climate commitments, this future-proofing is increasingly important.

By pairing long-range capability with strong efficiency and lower emissions, the A321XLR allows airlines to grow their networks more responsibly. It enables new nonstop routes and better connectivity without a proportional increase in environmental impact. In this sense, the aircraft reflects a broader industry shift away from simply operating larger aircraft and toward smarter fleet choices that balance reach, efficiency, and sustainability.

Harmonising certification, registries, and sustainability standards in line with International Civil Aviation Organization across Asia-Pacific is critical for trust and interoperability.

The event brought together over 60 participants across government, industry, investors, and experts from Asia-Pacific and beyond, where we aimed to tackle a critical question: how do we scale SAF feedstock supply in Asia-Pacific from potential to bankable reality? ✈️Airbus framed the SAF supply chain as "The Invisible Engine" — with 1.9 Mt consumed in 2025 but 300 Mt needed by 2050, the capacity gap is massive. ASEAN alone holds at least 26.3Mt of SAF production potential, exceeding the region's 2019 jet fuel consumption of ~22.5Mt. 📊 Argus Media spotlighted shifting global feedstock trade flows — APAC's renewable diesel and SAF surplus can cover Europe's deficit until 2030, with the region set to process a greater proportion of feedstock domestically. New price benchmarks signal a maturing Asian market. 🌿 FatHopes Energy showcased how Southeast Asia's leading waste oil aggregator is advancing from feedstock collection to SAF refining — with a greenfield HEFA plant planned at Port Klang, Malaysia, backed by ISCC-compliant digital traceability. 🎙️ Our panel — Scaling SAF Feedstocks in Asia-Pacific: From Potential to Bankable Supply — featured representatives from Airbus, Neste, Apical, S&P Global Energy, and Aether Fuels, tackling feedstock diversification beyond Used Cooking Oil, supply chain logistics costs, and the role of book-and-claim mechanisms. Five key takeaways emerged: 1. Harmonising certification, registries, and sustainability standards in line with International Civil Aviation Organization across Asia-Pacific is critical for trust and interoperability. 2. Building national frameworks with reference to CORSIA and integrating SAF into net-zero and ETS policies globally. 3. Implementing SAF blending mandates with Book-and-Claim frameworks balances scale and sustainability while providing airline flexibility. 4. Supporting waste oil collection, agricultural residues, and novel feedstocks like algae is crucial for diversifying the resource base. 5. Backing advanced pathways (AtJ, Fischer-Tropsch, PtL) through grants and fast permitting will help move beyond HEFA by 2030. The message was clear: the feedstock is there, but building bankable supply chains requires coordinated action across policy, technology, and finance. We would like to thank our sponsor, Airbus, for making this event possible, and our speakers and panellists for their contributions. The discussions underscored the real-world complexities of scaling SAF and reinforced the importance of open, cross-sector dialogue to surface challenges. description:
Australian low-carbon fuel company HAMR Energy has successfully closed a USD$7 million Series A funding round, securing significant investment from aviation leaders Airbus and Qantas, alongside industrial giant thyssenkrupp Uhde.
Strategic Investment to Fuel Decarbonisation The investment from Qantas and Airbus stems from their joint Australian Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) fund, underscoring a strong industry commitment to fostering a local clean energy ecosystem.
HAMR Energy has developed a vertically integrated production model to ensure a consistent and sustainable supply chain.

text:
Australian low-carbon fuel company HAMR Energy has successfully closed a USD$7 million Series A funding round, securing significant investment from aviation leaders Airbus and Qantas, alongside industrial giant thyssenkrupp Uhde. This capital injection is set to accelerate the development of projects that convert plantation forestry residues into sustainable fuels. The initiative marks a critical step in decarbonising the hard-to-abate aviation and shipping sectors while bolstering Australia's domestic energy security.

Strategic Investment to Fuel Decarbonisation

The investment from Qantas and Airbus stems from their joint Australian Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) fund, underscoring a strong industry commitment to fostering a local clean energy ecosystem. This funding is pivotal for HAMR Energy as it advances its pipeline of low-carbon liquid fuel projects. The collaboration aims to establish a reliable supply of sustainable fuels, which is essential for meeting long-term emissions reduction targets in global transport.

At the forefront of these plans is the company's flagship project, Portland Renewable Fuels (PRF), located in regional Victoria. The facility is designed to process residues from the local plantation forestry industry to produce 300,000 tonnes of low-carbon methanol annually. This methanol offers a versatile solution, capable of being used directly as a marine fuel or converted into sustainable aviation fuel to address the industry's growing needs.

Pioneering Technology and Partnerships

A key technological component will be provided by Honeywell, which will license its innovative UOP eFining™ process technology. This marks the first commercial application of the technology in the Pacific region, enabling the efficient conversion of methanol into renewable aviation fuel. This partnership provides a proven and scalable pathway for producing SAF, representing a major breakthrough for the Australian aviation industry's sustainability goals.

HAMR Energy has developed a vertically integrated production model to ensure a consistent and sustainable supply chain. The company has secured Memorandums of Understanding for long-term feedstock supply with partners like sustainable forestry company OneFortyOne. This strategy leverages existing byproducts, offering lower lifecycle emissions than alternative feedstocks and ensuring the production of highly competitive low-carbon fuels.

Projected Economic and Environmental Impact

With global SAF demand projected to reach 500 million tonnes by 2050, HAMR Energy's projects are positioned to play a crucial role in meeting this target. By establishing a domestic production capability, Australia can help close the significant global supply gap. This move not only supports international climate objectives but also significantly enhances the nation's resilience and self-sufficiency in aviation fuel supply.

The development of a domestic SAF sector is expected to deliver substantial economic benefits, according to a study commissioned by Qantas and Airbus. The report estimates the industry could contribute USD$9.3 billion in Gross Value Add to the Australian economy. Furthermore, it is projected to create or sustain as many as 70,000 jobs, stimulating economic growth and regional development across the country.

In addition to the PRF project, HAMR Energy is developing Australia’s first large-scale methanol-to-jet fuel facility. This plant will have the capacity to produce over 135 million litres of SAF annually. The project is expected to create hundreds of construction jobs and up to 130 permanent operational roles, providing a significant boost to regional employment and industry expertise.

This strategic funding round represents a landmark moment for Australia's emerging renewable fuels industry. The powerful collaboration between HAMR Energy, aviation giants, and technology leaders provides a clear pathway to producing low-carbon fuels at scale. The initiative is poised to deliver significant environmental benefits while driving economic growth and establishing Australia as a key contributor to the global energy transition.

text:
GEVO, Inc. (NASDAQ: GEVO), a leader in renewable fuels and chemicals, as well as carbon management, today announced the successful closing of a refinancing transaction on February 6, 2026 that simplifies the company's capital structure. As part of the transaction, GEVO redeemed all existing tranches of bonds relating to its renewable natural gas ("RNG") subsidiary, which totaled approximately $68 million. The bond redemptions allowed GEVO to free up more than $35 million of previously restricted cash without a material change to the company's total outstanding debt and with lower administrative costs.

The $175 million loan facility with Orion Infrastructure Capital ("OIC") consolidates the existing Gevo North Dakota term debt with the debt associated with Gevo's RNG subsidiary. In addition, on February 6, 2026, Gevo entered a revolving credit facility of up to $20 million with Huntington National Bank. This flexible credit provides working capital for Gevo's low-carbon ethanol plant operations at Gevo North Dakota.

About Gevo

Gevo is a next-generation diversified energy company committed to fueling America's future with cost-effective, drop-in fuels that contribute to energy security, abate carbon, and strengthen rural communities to drive economic growth. Gevo's innovative technology can be used to make a variety of renewable products, including sustainable aviation fuel ("SAF"), motor fuels, chemicals, and other materials that provide U.S.-made solutions. Gevo's business model includes developing, financing, and operating production facilities that create jobs and revitalize communities. Gevo owns and operates an ethanol plant with an adjacent carbon capture and sequestration ("CCS") facility and Class VI carbon-storage well. Gevo also owns and operates one of the largest dairy-based renewable natural gas ("RNG") facilities in the United States, turning by-products into clean, reliable energy. Additionally, Gevo developed the world's first production facility for specialty alcohol-to-jet ("ATJ") fuels and chemicals operating since 2012. Gevo is currently developing the world's first large-scale ATJ facility to be co-located at our North Dakota site. Gevo's market-driven "pay-for-performance" approach regarding carbon and other sustainability attributes helps deliver value to our local economies. Through its Verity subsidiary, Gevo provides transparency, accountability, and efficiency in tracking, measuring, and verifying various attributes throughout the supply chain. By strengthening rural economies, Gevo is working to secure a self-sufficient future and to make sure value is brought to the market.

For more information, see www.gevo.com.

text:
Biomass Magazine announced the general session speakers for the 2026 International Biomass Conference & Expo, scheduled for March 31 to April 2, 2026, at the Gaylord Opryland Resort & Convention Center in Nashville, Tennessee. The general session will be held Wed., April 1, and will feature the presentation of three industry awards, a keynote presentation, followed by a moderated roundtable discussion focused on biochar and biocarbon market development.

The general session keynote presentation will be delivered by Rudi Roeslein, founder of Roeslein Alternative Energy, whose work has advanced integrated biomass, renewable energy and carbon-negative project development across the U.S.

“Nashville continues to be an outstanding host city for the International Biomass Conference & Expo, and the momentum we’re seeing for 2026 reflects both the strength of the market and the caliber of the speakers and sessions” said John Nelson, chief operating officer of BBI International and Biomass Magazine. “With a keynote from Rudi Roeslein and a general session lineup that brings together leaders shaping the future of biochar, biocarbon and carbon-negative systems, the content this year is exceptional. Registration and exhibitor interest are pacing well ahead of expectations, underscoring the industry’s enthusiasm for the conversations and opportunities this event delivers.”

Following the keynote, the program will feature a 65-minute Biochar and Biocarbon Roundtable discussion.

Roundtable participants include:

Moderator Myles Gray, executive director, U.S. Biochar Initiative

John Teal, vice president of commercial, TSI, and board chairman, International Biomass Torrefaction and Carbonization Council

Andrew White, cofounder and CEO, CHAR Technologies

John-Paul Taylor, founding partner, KindlePoint Partners

Kevin Hatman, U.S. communications and trust manager, Carbonfuture

The discussion will explore the evolving role of biocarbon and biochar in carbon-negative energy systems, market development, investment considerations and integration across the broader biomass value chain. Audience members will be able to engage directly with panelists by asking questions from the floor or submitting questions electronically during the session.

“This year’s general session reflects where the biomass industry is headed,” said Anna Simet, senior editor and director of content at BBI International. “With biocarbon and biochar moving from niche applications toward scalable, investable solutions, the conversation around market development, integration and carbon value has never been more timely. Bringing together this group of speakers allows us to explore those dynamics in a practical, forward-looking way.”

In addition to the general session, industry association executives will host breakout discussions following general session, bringing together producers and co-panelists for more targeted, in-depth conversations.

These breakout discussions will feature:

Tim Portz, executive director, Pellet Fuels Institute

Darrell Smith, executive director, U.S. Industrial Pellet Association

Carrie Annand, executive director, American Biomass Energy Association

Patrick Serfass, executive director, American Biogas Council

Kurt Kovarik, vice president of federal affairs, Clean Fuels Alliance America

Registration for the 2026 International Biomass Conference & Expo provides access to all conference programming at one price, including sessions from all co-located events, plenary and breakout sessions, entry to the Expo, meal functions and speaker presentations available through the conference app during the event. Biomass producers are eligible to attend at no cost.

The 2026 International Biomass Conference & Expo, co-located with the North American Biocarbon Conference, will take place March 31–April 2 in Nashville, Tennessee.

For more information and registration details, visit https://2026-ibce.bbiconferences.com.

About Biomass Magazine

Biomass Magazine is a quarterly trade publication tailored to serve companies and organizations engaged in producing or utilizing biomass power and heat, advanced biofuels, biogas, wood pellets and biobased chemicals. In addition to policy, regulation, project finance, technology, and plant management, the publication maintains a core editorial focus on biomass logistics. Its international readership includes owners and managers of biomass power, CHP, and district heating facilities; pellet manufacturing plant owners and managers; professionals working in captive feedstock industries-from food processing and waste management to agriculture and forest products manufacturing-and a growing number of industrial manufacturers, municipal decision makers, researchers, and technology providers engaged in biomass utilization in 40 countries. Biomass Magazine is committed to editorial excellence and high-quality print production and distribution.

About BBI International

BBI International is the world’s leading provider of acclaimed events and highly informative trade magazines within the energy sector. Our portfolio boasts a diverse range of industry-focused conferences and expos, including the renowned International Fuel Ethanol Workshop & Expo, the largest and longest-running ethanol conference in the world. Alongside this flagship event, we own and operate key summits such as the UAS Summit & Expo, Sustainable Fuels Summit, International Biomass Conference & Expo, North American SAF Conference & Expo, and the Carbon Capture & Storage Summit, as well as a series of influential regional events. Our publishing arm extends our expertise into print and online content, with a collection of industry-defining magazines including Ethanol Producer Magazine, Biomass Magazine, Pellet Mill Magazine, Biodiesel Magazine, UAS Magazine, SAF Magazine, and Carbon Capture Magazine. These publications, together with an array of ancillary products including maps, directories, e-newsletters, and other digital resources, underscore our commitment to providing comprehensive, up-to-date information and insights to professionals in the bioenergy sectors, including ethanol, biomass, pellets, biogas, RNG, sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), biodiesel & renewable diesel and as well as the UAS industry, which focuses on unmanned aerial systems.

title: Green light for green flight: The UK's new SAF revenue certainty mechanism explained
description:
Aviation’s race to net zero is gaining altitude, powered by Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF).
Following the 2025 launch of the SAF Mandate, which secures a guaranteed level of demand, the UK is now delivering the final piece of the puzzle - the Revenue Certainty Mechanism (RCM).
But how will this new scheme reshape investment, and what does it mean for the future of flight?
INTRODUCTION SAF plays a central role in reducing fossil fuel emissions during flight, and improving its production and usage is a core target for the current Government.
Last year saw the introduction of the SAF Mandate, a key policy mechanism which establishes demand by requiring a certain percentage of SAF to be used for any flight.
text:
Aviation’s race to net zero is gaining altitude, powered by Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF). Following the 2025 launch of the SAF Mandate, which secures a guaranteed level of demand, the UK is now delivering the final piece of the puzzle - the Revenue Certainty Mechanism (RCM). But how will this new scheme reshape investment, and what does it mean for the future of flight?

INTRODUCTION

SAF plays a central role in reducing fossil fuel emissions during flight, and improving its production and usage is a core target for the current Government. Last year saw the introduction of the SAF Mandate, a key policy mechanism which establishes demand by requiring a certain percentage of SAF to be used for any flight. Furthermore, the SAF Bill, which enters the Report Stage on 4 February 2026 and is expected to receive Royal Assent this year, provides the legislative powers to underpin the RCM and gives further clarity to aircraft operators.

title: Charleroi Airport completes major lighting upgrade to enhance runway safety
description:
Charleroi Airport completes major lighting upgrade to enhance runway safety 0 SHARES Posted: 11 February 2026 | Gabriel Higgins | Brussels South Charleroi Airport has finalised a comprehensive LED lighting modernisation, strengthening runway safety, reliability and energy efficiency without disrupting operations.
Credit: SPIE Belgium Brussels South Charleroi Airport (CRL) has completed a major multi year programme to modernise its airfield lighting infrastructure, significantly enhancing runway safety, operational reliability and energy performance.
Since 2018, the airport has overseen the complete renewal of its lighting power supply networks.
Given that Brussels South Charleroi Airport operates a single runway, works were carried out almost exclusively at night within restricted time windows to avoid disruption to daily commercial flights.
With the completion of the programme, covered by a five year guarantee including preventive and corrective maintenance, Brussels South Charleroi Airport has strengthened both safety standards and long term operational performance while reducing its environmental footprint.
text:
Charleroi Airport completes major lighting upgrade to enhance runway safety

0

SHARES

Posted: 11 February 2026 | Gabriel Higgins |

Brussels South Charleroi Airport has finalised a comprehensive LED lighting modernisation, strengthening runway safety, reliability and energy efficiency without disrupting operations.

Credit: SPIE Belgium

Brussels South Charleroi Airport (CRL) has completed a major multi year programme to modernise its airfield lighting infrastructure, significantly enhancing runway safety, operational reliability and energy performance.

Delivered in partnership with SOWAER and executed by SPIE Belgium, the project represents one of the most significant technical upgrades undertaken at the Belgian airport in recent years. Although largely invisible to passengers, airfield lighting is fundamental to safe aircraft movements, guiding aircraft during taxiing, take off and landing.

Since 2018, the airport has overseen the complete renewal of its lighting power supply networks. Around 200 kilometres of underground cabling have been replaced, including 5,000 volt primary high voltage cables, 48 volt secondary cables and earth cables, together with several thousand connectors. The next generation cables, supplied in cooperation with Kabelwerk Eupen, are designed to provide an estimated service life of 15 to 20 years, reinforcing long term infrastructure resilience.

A key milestone in the programme is the transition to LED technology. Approximately 95 per cent of the airport’s lighting is now LED based, with 2,650 lights and panels installed. This transformation has reduced energy consumption by between 80 and 85 per cent, lowering demand from around 152 kW to approximately 25 kW. LED units consume five to eight times less energy than the previous halogen fittings and offer a service life two to three times longer, significantly improving asset longevity and availability.

Operational reliability has also improved markedly. Since the renovation of the upgraded circuits, no technical incidents have been recorded, whereas previous cable faults occurred several times annually.

Given that Brussels South Charleroi Airport operates a single runway, works were carried out almost exclusively at night within restricted time windows to avoid disruption to daily commercial flights. Specialised teams implemented temporary technical solutions to ensure the infrastructure was fully operational each morning.

With the completion of the programme, covered by a five year guarantee including preventive and corrective maintenance, Brussels South Charleroi Airport has strengthened both safety standards and long term operational performance while reducing its environmental footprint.

title: Sustainable Aviation Fuel: On the Flight Path to Revenue Certainty – Part 2 - Burges Salmon
description:
Last month the Department for Transport (DfT) launched a consultation on its approach to delivering the SAF revenue certainty mechanism (RCM).
Having assessed several allocation models, the Government’s minded to position for SAF AR1 is to use a tendered bid process with bilateral negotiations.
It is not yet clear when SAF AR1 will be launched or how long the application window will be open – but we expect to find detail on this when the SAF AR1 applicant guidance is published in due course.
Contracts awarded Following BAFO, projects will be awarded RCM contracts where the government is satisfied with the agreed terms and strike pricing.
It is not currently clear the number of contracts which the government anticipate offering as part of SAF AR1.
text:
Last month the Department for Transport (DfT) launched a consultation on its approach to delivering the SAF revenue certainty mechanism (RCM).

Our previous article at the end of January set out our view of 5 of the key areas to consider in relation to the proposed terms for the RCM contracts. In this article, we look at the government’s “minded to” position for how those contracts will be allocated for the first allocation round, known as SAF AR1. As a reminder, the consultation closes on 3 April 2026.

So what’s being proposed?

The market has seen allocation models before – for example, in nuclear/other low-carbon electricity Contracts for Difference and Hydrogen. Having assessed several allocation models, the Government’s minded to position for SAF AR1 is to use a tendered bid process with bilateral negotiations. This process will consist of a number of stages, which are outlined in more detail below. Much will be familiar to those with knowledge of existing allocation round models:

1. Application Window

Applicants will be able to submit bids at any time during the application window period. It is not yet clear when SAF AR1 will be launched or how long the application window will be open – but we expect to find detail on this when the SAF AR1 applicant guidance is published in due course. The guidance will also include information on how to submit bids and what documentation will be required.

2. Eligibility Checks

All applications will be assessed on a pass/fail basis against pre-defined eligibility criteria designed to minimise speculative applications and speed up the process to focus on the most deliverable projects which will produce SAF which is eligible for SAF Mandate certificates.

The Government’s minded to position is that these criteria will include:

Location: The plant will need to be in the UK and the applicant a UK registered company or charity.

Delivery Date: Confirmation that the project will begin supplying SAF in a specified delivery year, aligned with its planned commissioning timetable, so government has ability to allocate budget to projects best able to support SAF Mandate targets. Projects will be expected to deliver on key milestone information provided at the eligibility stage.

Certification: Evidence that the technologies used to produce the SAF can meet the relevant international standards for aviation use (ASTM International standard D1655/D7566, the Ministry of Defence standard 91-091, or equivalent), whether the fuel is used on its own or blended with fossil aviation fuel.

Technology Type: All non-HEFA technologies are eligible.

Capacity: A minimum installed production capacity of 20 kilotonnes per annum (ktpa).

New-Build: Only new-build SAF production facilities will be supported. However, new capacity developed at existing sites where at least 20 ktpa of additional SAF production is constructed and co-processing of non-HEFA SAF within conventional petroleum refineries is proposed to be eligible.

SAF Mandate Compliance: Projects must show that produced SAF will comply with all SAF Mandate requirements and qualify for certificates, including minimum lifecycle GHG savings (per the GHG intensity methodologies already set out in the RFTO/SAF Mandate technical guidance), sustainability criteria and feedstock eligibility.

Feedstock Supply: Projects must evidence engagement with feedstock providers (“including but not limited to written agreements such as Memorandums of Understanding) to supply feedstock over the project lifetime, including detailed sourcing arrangements and sustainability assurances.

Power Supply: Evidence of a grid connection offer, or other written evidence that the project will have access to power supply by the Target Commissioning Date.

Engineering Design Phase: Applicants must have completed at least a FEL‑1 (front-end loading) feasibility study, including an initial technical and economic assessment to confirm project viability. This will need to define: production concept, key technology options, regulatory and planning considerations, land options, indicative site layout, feedstock and utility requirements, offtake and routes to market including plans for SAF transport, storage and blending, and preliminary cost and schedule estimates.

Access to Finance: Evidence of finance, including letters of support from equity/debt providers and/or confirmation of ability to fund from existing sources.

3. Evaluation Criteria

Projects which pass the eligibility check will then move onto the evaluation phase. This phase is a “holistic assessment” of applications based on deliverability, normalised strike price and economic benefits. Each of the evaluation criteria has been allocated a weighting and the application’s final total will be calculated using the final scores for each criterion multiplied by the associated weighting.

Deliverability (Weighting – 50%)

This criterion evaluates the applicant against several deliverability metrics including applicant’s organisational capability, the project’s current level of development and credibility of plans to complete all future development to reach commercial operations within the selected delivery year.

For this stage, a more comprehensive and detailed set of delivery metrics is proposed, supplementing the eligibility stage requirements set out above. The consultation does not set out how these metrics will be evaluated (but promises more detail in the SAF AR1 applicant guidance) save that scoring will reflect the “clarity and level of definition demonstrated…with bids scored higher if they can demonstrate greater organisational, project, technical and commercial deliverability”.

For SAF AR1 to be successful, it will be important to ensure that these metrics requirements can be meaningfully scored and that there is a clear distinction between the eligibility hurdle and the evaluation requirement.

Normalised Strike Price (Weighting – 40%)

This assesses projects on their cost-effectiveness in delivering carbon savings by comparing the price of SAF per tonne of carbon dioxide avoided relative to fossil fuel, adjusted for differences in technology, feedstock and lifecycle GHG performance. The strike price and GHG emissions savings submitted with a bid must reflect the project’s expected performance at the Target Commissioning Date (rather than projected future savings) and projects with a lower normalised strike price will score higher, reflecting better value for money.

Economic Benefits (Weighting – 10%)

This criterion assesses a project’s contribution to the UK economy, including job creation and wider economic benefits, alongside its carbon reduction impact. It reflects the role of the SAF Mandate in supporting the development of a sustainable, long‑term UK SAF industry capable of delivering skilled employment and investment.

4. Shortlisting

Following the evaluation stage, a longlist of projects with the highest evaluation scores will be assessed against how well their project aligns with the strategic allocation objectives (see below). Further detail on scoring thresholds, minimum scores and the number of projects to be longlisted or shortlisted is not provided in the consultation and is expected to be set out in the SAF AR1 applicant guidance.

However, where the government considers that the round is oversubscribed, or that evaluation results have created a portfolio imbalance across technology, feedstock, delivery timing, size or location, the government may apply portfolio factors and select lower‑scoring projects where this improves overall balance. The consultation states that the government retains full discretion on any “imbalance” decision - a primary driver for the SAF AR1 “tendered bid” approach is to reduce over-reliance on a single pathway and ensure different production technologies and feedstocks are supported.

This should therefore be a particular area of focus for prospective developers to respond to as part of the consultation. Developers should also start to consider how they will distinguish their project from others who may be applying to SAF AR1, particularly if their proposed technology is similar to a number of other projects, to ensure they come out on the right side of such a decision should the government adopt this position.

5. Due Diligence

Shortlisted applications will be invited to progress to an open book due diligence assessment to verify the key technical and commercial assumptions, through detailed costs and assurance assessment, compliance checks to assess lifecycle carbon intensity of the SAF to be produced as well as ‘know your customer’ (KYC) style checks.

6. Agreeing an offer

Applicants who pass the due diligence stage will engage directly with government to agree an offer for RCM support, with a focus on the key contract terms of the front-end agreement such as the strike price and target commissioning date. Following conclusion of these engagements, applicants will be asked to submit Best and Final Offer (BAFO).

7. Contracts awarded

Following BAFO, projects will be awarded RCM contracts where the government is satisfied with the agreed terms and strike pricing. The consultation does not explain how BAFOs will be assessed or how final contract‑award decisions will be made, with this detail also expected to follow in the SAF AR1 applicant guidance. It is not currently clear the number of contracts which the government anticipate offering as part of SAF AR1.

The Overall Allocation Strategy

As well as outlining the approach for AR1, the consultation summarises the government’s “minded to” positions on developing an overall allocation strategy for RCM contracts to provide further long-term clarity. At present, the overall allocation strategy is expected to include:

Indicative volumes and associated GHG emission reductions expected over the life of the RCM scheme;

How the volumes will be distributed across the various allocation rounds (and a number of expected allocation rounds)

The anticipated format for allocation rounds

Indicative timeframe for RCM support for the industry

The approach to support different technologies and feedstocks during the RCM.

Whilst the government is presently focusing on the tendered bid with bilateral negotiation process for AR1, the consultation highlights that – if the market matures – there would be benefits in transitioning towards a price-based allocation for future rounds to drive longer-term value for money and ensure GHG emission reductions are supported..

Next steps

It is expected that the overall allocation strategy, as well as an indicative timeline for SAF AR1, will be published during 2026 once the government has considered stakeholder responses to the consultation.

Given that obtaining an RCM contract will likely play an important role in deciding which developers (at least in the near term) are able to secure finance for their projects, it is important that all stakeholders understand and provide input on the proposed allocation approach now. Nothing is decided and DfT intends to appoint a delivery partner to support the detailed design of the allocation process in the near future.

The consultation closes on 3 April 202 and the government anticipates that the required legislation to implement the RCM will be laid by the end of 2026, including any associated regulations.

If you would like support from us in providing a response to the consultation, or gaining a better understanding of the proposed approach for SAF AR1, then please contact Nick Churchward, Greg Fearn or your usual Burges Salmon contact.

This article was written by Nick Churchward, Greg Fearn, Sasha Anisman, Ellen Matthews and Ishbel McCormack.

title: Fully flexible - The Business Travel Magazine
description:
Allegris is designed to deliver a highly personalised flying experience, backed by a €2.5 billion investment across all four long-haul classes: Economy, Premium Economy, Business and First.
Business Class offers five seat types to suit a range of requirements.
The Business Class Suite, with its front-row position, high partitions and door, is ideal for work or rest.
Business Class Suites have been especially well received, with executives praising the ability to work or rest in privacy.
Early results are strong: 90% of passengers say they would choose Lufthansa Allegris again, a clear endorsement of the new product.
text:
What is unique about the new Lufthansa Allegris?

Allegris is designed to deliver a highly personalised flying experience, backed by a €2.5 billion investment across all four long-haul classes: Economy, Premium Economy, Business and First.

In First Class, Suite Plus introduces single and double suites that function like private rooms, complete with closable doors, dining tables and fully flat beds. Business Class offers five seat types to suit a range of requirements. The Business Class Suite, with its front-row position, high partitions and door, is ideal for work or rest. Two individual suites can become a double to cater to the growing ‘bleisure’ trend, while the Extra-space seat provides additional work surface for productivity. Premium Economy, meanwhile, balances value and comfort with generous seat pitch, wireless charging and power sockets – perfect for cost-conscious corporates working on the go.

How is the rollout going?

To date, 10 Allegris-equipped A350-900s are flying from Munich to destinations including Bengaluru, Chicago, Cape Town, Miami, Newark, New York, Shanghai and Tokyo-Haneda.

The second phase has begun with four B787-9 currently operating from Frankfurt. Four more aircraft will join Frankfurt by year-end, and by summer 2026 Lufthansa expects to have 20 B787-9s in service. Next year will also see the arrival of the A350-1000 and retrofitting of the first 747-8, further expanding the Allegris fleet.

What has the feedback been so far?

Business Class Suites have been especially well received, with executives praising the ability to work or rest in privacy. Corporate customers value the flexibility Allegris provides, enabling tailored travel options that align with policy and budget. Early results are strong: 90% of passengers say they would choose Lufthansa Allegris again, a clear endorsement of the new product.

Why is the flexibility of Lufthansa Allegris so important to your corporate clients?

As Lufthansa approaches its 100th anniversary, one lesson stands out: no two customers are alike. Allegris reflects that insight by offering choice and adaptability. For corporate travellers, the product ensures trips meet their purpose, whether for privacy, collaboration or rest.

Wellbeing is now a major corporate priority. Companies know staff perform best when they feel valued and rested, and Lufthansa aims to support that by creating environments that balance productivity with comfort.

What else is Lufthansa doing to cater for the corporate market?

For corporates, a primary requirement of any airline partnership is simplicity. The PartnerPlusBenefit programme lets companies earn points on every ticket, redeemable for flights, upgrades, services and sustainable aviation fuel.

The new Cash & Points option allows partial payment with points, simplifying budget management. In parallel, our Miles & More frequent flyer programme allows members to accrue personal miles, making the scheme straightforward and popular.

With all eyes on emission reporting, our Green Fares automatically include CO2 offsetting – 20% through Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) and 80% via certified climate projects for European flights (10% SAF on long-haul). Tickets also come with up to 20% more miles and points and free rebooking, delivering tangible benefits alongside environmental responsibility.
